Passion Novels - Obsessive ML
Publicidad
"I want to mark you tonight." I choked on my wine. Smooth. "That's— What?" "We're mated, Blake. Have been since our wedding night." His voice was steady, certain. "But I want it official. Permanent. Before..." "Before my parents arrive and everything goes to hell?" The words tasted bitter. "Before anything." He cupped my face, thumb brushing my cheekbone. "Because you're mine, and I'm yours, and I want the whole world to know it." My throat felt suspiciously tight. "That's the cheesiest thing you've ever said." "Is that a ‘yes’?" "Like you even have to ask." I leaned into his touch. "Yes, you possessive wolf. ‘Yes’ to all of it." The way back to our bedroom was charged with anticipation, neither of us speaking but both very aware of what came next.         Chapter 1 POV Blake You know what's worse than being the family disappointment?  Being reminded of it over salmon while your parents dissect your genetic failures like you're not sitting right fucking there. I'm parked at the ass-end of our mahogany table, close enough to hear every insult but far enough away that I might as well be dining in a different time zone. "The Blackwood pack called again about Amelia's engagement announcement," my father says, slicing his steak with the precision of a serial killer. "They want an invitation to our glorious daughter's wedding." "Of course they do.” My mother practically orgasms at the mention. “Amelia represents everything our bloodline stands for—strength, intelligence, ability that could rival the Moon Goddess herself." And here it comes. Wait for it. Three, two, one— "It's remarkable, really.” Father's eyes slide to me like I'm dog shit he just noticed on his shoe. “Identical twins, but only one inherited anything worthwhile. Twenty years old and still no wolf. No powers. Not even basic pack instincts or mind link. What a pathetic creature..." I keep my face blank while mentally adding this to my running tally of creative insults. This one's actually pretty tame, last week he called me a "genetic typo." "Father, that's not fair…" Amelia tries. "Fair?" Mother's laugh could etch glass. "What's not fair is that the Moon Goddess played some cosmic joke, putting all the good genetics in one daughter and leaving us with..."  She waves at me like I'm a particularly disappointing science experiment.  "Whatever this is." I stab a piece of salmon. Whatever this is?  I'm your daughter, you designer-heel-wearing psychopath. But sure, let's go with "whatever." Father leans back, studying me with the warmth of a mortician examining a corpse.  "Do you know what the other packs say about us, Blake? They whisper that we're weak because we produced a defective heir. That maybe, our bloodline is tainted." Oh good, we've moved from personal attacks to pack politics. Progress. "Every day you exist is a reminder of our shame," he continues, casual as a weather report. "The weakest she-wolf, Alpha’s heir, in pack history. A genetic dead end." The words land like they always do—sharp, precise, designed to wound. But I've built up twenty years of scar tissue. What's a few more cuts? Before Father can launch into Act Two of Blake's Greatest Failures, Amelia stands abruptly.  "Evening training starts in fifteen minutes. Blake and I should go prepare." "Run along, then.” Father waves his fork dismissively. “Maybe she will discover a personality trait worth passing down." We escape the dining room, and I can feel Amelia's sympathy radiating off her like body heat. The silence stretches until she finally breaks. "I'm so sorry. They shouldn't—" "They're right," I cut her off, not meeting her eyes. "I'm the weakest one in the whole pack. Maybe the whole damn bloodline." I stop walking, forcing myself to look at her perfect face—my face, but somehow better.  "But I can train. I can be useful. Not amazing, not powerful—just... not disgusting." "You're not—" "Thanks," I interrupt again, because if she finishes that sentence with some motivational poster bullshit, I might actually lose it. "For getting me out of there." I jog ahead before she can respond, heading for the gym. The pack training facility is buzzing with young wolves finishing their drills. I throw myself into solo combat routines, channeling every ounce of frustration into strikes and blocks.  My body screams, lungs burn, muscles shake, but I keep pushing. Pain is just weakness leaving the body, or whatever toxic masculinity phrase the trainers love spouting. I'm cooling down, thinking I might actually survive another day, when two wolves from the younger training group slide into the ring. They circle me like I'm prey, which, let's be honest, in their eyes, I probably am. "Well, look who's trying to play soldier," the first one sneers.  His name's Mark, I think. Or Mason. Some M-name that screams 'future douchebag warrior.' "She doesn't even have a wolf," his buddy adds, laughing like he just invented comedy. "You think you can spar with real Nightshades?" I hold my stance, exhausted but not backing down. "One of you wants to test that, or both?" Mikey, let’s name him that, lunges first, and holy shit, he's fast. His fist connects with my face before I can fully dodge. I taste copper—blood in my mouth. At last, I manage to land one solid hit that surprises him, but then his friend jumps in because apparently honor is optional when you're beating on the pack’s rejects. They work me over like professionals.  My body hits the mat again and again, and the spectators eat it up, laughing and shouting encouragement. Because nothing says pack unity like watching the Alpha's defective daughter get her ass kicked. Then Amelia storms in like an avenging angel. She sends both wolves flying across the ring with a force that makes the building shake. One of them, still mid-swing at me, accidentally clips her cheek before getting launched. The moment he realizes what he's done, his face goes white.  You don't touch the Golden Child. That's like, rule number one. "Oh shit… Sorry, Amelia, I didn't mean—" "I'm fine," she says through gritted teeth, but her hand flies to her cheek and comes away bloody. "It's nothing." "Come on," Amelia mutters, already hauling me toward the exit of the gym. "We need to get some ice on this." By the time we reach my room, I can already feel tomorrow's bruises settling in. My ribs ache, my shoulder's definitely gonna be purple, and there's a nice split on my lip. But it's Amelia's cheek that has my stomach churning. That night, I'm taking inventory of my latest collection of colorful marks when Mother's silhouette appeared in my doorway. She doesn't knock. Never does. Her eagle eyes zero in on the small patch covering Amelia's cheek instantly. Of course. The woman could spot a microscopic imperfection from orbit. "What. Happened." Each word drops like ice. When I explain, she doesn't demand names. Doesn't promise the punishment for the idiots who ganged up on me. Instead, her hand tangles in my hair and yanks my head back so hard my neck pops. The slap that follows makes my ears ring and my vision white out for a solid three seconds. "How dare you," she hisses. "Your sister has a wedding in two weeks! Two. Weeks. And now she has to walk down the aisle covering up a bruise because you were too pathetic to defend yourself!" "Mom, stop—" Amelia tries from the hallway. But Mother's already dragging me through the house by my hair like I'm some rabid animal that needs to be put down.  She throws open the front doors with enough force to rattle the hinges, revealing a night so cold my breath fogs instantly. The full moon hangs overhead like a cosmic middle finger. "Shift," she orders, shoving me onto the stone steps. "Shift right now, or stay out here until sunrise. Maybe if you beg the Moon Goddess hard enough, she'll take pity on your worthless hide." The door slams with the finality of a coffin lid. I stay on my knees for a long moment, staring up at that bright, mocking sphere of rock that's supposed to be sacred to our kind. My jaw clenches so hard I might actually crack a tooth. But I don't cry and don't beg. I look toward the dark tree line beyond our perfect little courtyard, my fingers curling into fists until my nails draw blood from my palms. For the thousandth time, I reach deep inside myself, calling for the wolf that should be there.  Calling for the part of me that would make me worthy, make me whole, make me something more than the family's greatest disappointment. For the thousandth time, no one answers.� Chapter 2 POV Blake Morning comes with Amelia banging on my door like she's the FBI.  "Get up, we're going for a walk." I'm still treating my last night's bruises when she barges in anyway. Privacy is a myth when you have a twin with Alpha tendencies. "Jesus, Blake." She sees the damage and her face does that thing where she's simultaneously pissed and guilty. "That’s even more bad than I expected. Let me heal—" "Nope." I roll out of bed, ignoring how every muscle screams in protest. "Badge of honor. Plus, Mom will lose her shit if you waste healing energy on the family pet project." "You're not—" "Save it for someone who believes you." She follows me anyway. Because Amelia's got this pathological need to fix things, and I'm basically a walking disaster that never stays fixed. I grab my hoodie and head for the back door, not bothering to check if she's coming. The soft thud of her footsteps behind me anyway says she is. Twenty minutes later, we're deep in the forest behind pack territory, heading for our spot. The ancient oak grove is the one place on this whole fucking compound where I can breathe without calculating how much space I'm allowed to take up. Amelia settles against our favorite tree, this massive tree that's probably older than our entire bloodline's superiority complex. I drop beside her, letting the quiet wash over us. No pack politics, no disappointed parents, no genetic comparisons. Just us. "This is all bullshit, you know," she says finally. "Gonna need you to be more specific, sis,” I snort. “The part where I'm a walking disappointment? Or the part where our parents treat me like a science experiment that went wrong?" "All of it." She's using her fierce voice, the one that makes lesser wolves submit. "They decided you were defective before you could even walk. Before you had a chance to—" "To what? Manifest my super special werewolf powers?" I pick at the oak's bark, avoiding her eyes. "I'm twenty and still waiting for my wolf to show up. Maybe the Moon Goddess just forgot about me. Left me on read, you know?" "Or maybe She has different timing." Amelia's conviction could move mountains, I swear. "You can mind-link with me, that's something." True. Our mental connection is literally the only supernatural party trick I've got.  While other wolves are shifting and showing off their dominance, I'm over here playing telepathic telephone with my sister. Fucking spectacular. ‘Stop that,’ her voice echoes in my head. ‘You're more than—’ ‘Than what?’ I shoot back through the link. ‘More than the family embarrassment? More than a genetic dead end? Face it, Am. I'm basically a really expensive human.’ She goes quiet, both out loud and in my head, which means she's thinking. Dangerous territory. "Speaking of expensive humans," I say, desperate to change the subject, "how's the whole arranged marriage thing treating you so far?" Her face does something complicated—not quite the look of impending doom I expected, but not exactly bridal bliss either.  "Alpha Matthias Henderson." She says the name like she's testing how it feels in her mouth.  And, surprisingly, it doesn't seem to taste like poison. "Father still thinks marrying me off to the Alpha of our biggest rival pack will create peace between us and give access to their territories."  She's talking about it like a prisoner recounting the hour of their execution. Inescapable and heavy with dread.  Amelia picks at the bark of our oak tree, not meeting my eyes. "But honestly? It could be worse. A lot worse." "That's the most ringing endorsement of an arranged marriage I've ever heard." "Shut up." But she's almost smiling.  "You've been meeting with him twice a year since we were sixteen," I begin carefully, "and you never tell me much about him. Maybe it's about time?" Amelia's quiet for a moment, and what bleeds through our link isn't the bone-deep terror I expected, but something more complicated. Resignation mixed with... relief?  "Matthias is actually pretty decent," she admits. "He's smart—like, genuinely intelligent, not just Alpha posturing. Has this dry sense of humor that catches me off guard sometimes." "Shocking. A wolf with a personality." "He gets it, you know? The political stuff. He's not thrilled about the arranged part either, but he's not taking it out on me. We've had some surprisingly honest conversations about what this whole thing actually means." That's... not what I expected. "So you actually like him?" "As a person? Yeah, I do. He's honorable, doesn't treat me like a trophy wife in waiting, and he's got this whole brooding Alpha thing that probably makes other she-wolves swoon."  She pauses. "But it's not love. We both know that." "But it's not horrible." "But it's not horrible," she agrees. "He's made it clear that he's not expecting some fairy tale romance. It's politics with a wedding dress, but at least he's not a complete asshole about it. And I'm a treaty with tits." "Poetic. You should put that in your vows." She elbows me, but gently. Always gently with me, like I might break.  Which, fair. I'm basically held together with spite and stubbornness at this point. "Two weeks," she whispers, and for the first time, it doesn't sound like a death sentence. "Two
